record.field: Slash is a self-hosted platform that turns long, unwieldy URLs into short, human-readable shortcuts you and your team can find and share instantly.

Slash is a self-hosted platform that turns long, unwieldy URLs into short, human-readable shortcuts you and your team can find and share instantly.

Slash lets you create memorable links such as s/shortcut in place of complex URLs, then organize them with tags and group them into shareable collections (c/collection). Built for teams that lose time hunting for the right link across emails, chats and docs, it centralizes frequently used links in one searchable place. A backend in Go with a React/TypeScript frontend keeps the deployment lightweight, and a browser extension lets you jump straight to a shortcut from the address bar.

Key features
  • Customizable, human-readable short links (s/shortcut) for any URL
  • Tagging and searchable organization of all your links
  • Collections to group and share sets of shortcuts publicly or with teammates
  • Visibility controls: keep links private, team-only, or public
  • Link traffic and source analytics
  • Browser extension (Chromium and Firefox) plus single-command Docker deployment
Best for

Teams and individuals who want to own a private link shortener and shortcut directory, replacing scattered links and SaaS go/ services with a self-hosted, taggable, shareable catalog of frequently used URLs.

services: slash-app: image: yourselfhosted/slash:latest volumes: - ./slash_app_data:/var/opt/slash environment: - SLASH_DRIVER=postgres - SLASH_DSN=postgresql://{{ db_user }}:{{ db_password }}@slash-postgres:5432/{{ db_name }}?sslmode=disable depends_on: slash-postgres: condition: service_healthy restart: unless-stopped container_name: '{{ INSTANCE_CODE }}' ports: - '{{ PORT_HTTP }}:5231' networks: - internal - agz-proxy-network slash-postgres: image: postgres:16-alpine volumes: - ./slash_postgres_data:/var/lib/postgresql/data environment: - POSTGRES_USER={{ db_user }} - POSTGRES_PASSWORD={{ db_password }} - POSTGRES_DB={{ db_name }} healthcheck: test: - CMD-SHELL - pg_isready -U {{ db_user }} -d {{ db_name }} interval: 10s timeout: 5s retries: 5 restart: unless-stopped container_name: '{{ INSTANCE_CODE }}_slash_postgres' networks: - internal networks: internal: driver: bridge agz-proxy-network: external: true

#!/bin/bash set -euo pipefail cd "$SERVICE_PATH" # Dump consistente de PostgreSQL (servicio en marcha) → $BACKUP_DATA_DIR docker compose exec -T slash-postgres sh -c 'pg_dump -U "$POSTGRES_USER" -d "$POSTGRES_DB" --no-owner -Fc' \ > "$BACKUP_DATA_DIR/db.dump" echo "pg_dump OK ($(wc -c < "$BACKUP_DATA_DIR/db.dump") bytes)"

#!/bin/bash set -euo pipefail cd "$SERVICE_PATH" # Levanta solo la DB, espera readiness y restaura docker compose up -d slash-postgres for i in $(seq 1 30); do docker compose exec -T slash-postgres sh -c 'pg_isready -U "$POSTGRES_USER"' && break sleep 2 done docker compose exec -T slash-postgres sh -c 'pg_restore -U "$POSTGRES_USER" -d "$POSTGRES_DB" --clean --if-exists --no-owner' \ < "$RESTORE_DATA_DIR/db.dump" # Verificación: la DB no quedó vacía (sh -c con dobles comillas; $VAR del contenedor con \$) N=$(docker compose exec -T slash-postgres sh -c "psql -U \$POSTGRES_USER -d \$POSTGRES_DB -tAc \"SELECT count(*) FROM information_schema.tables WHERE table_schema='public'\"") echo "tablas restauradas: $N" test "$N" -gt 0
